After digging into the landscape of AI content detection, one project stands out for doing it differently and better on Bittensor: @ai_detection (It's AI - SN32).

Most detectors today are centralized black boxes. You paste text, get a score, and hope the company behind it isnвҖҷt training on your data or tweaking thresholds for marketing reasons. ItвҖҷs opaque, single-point-of-failure prone, and often inconsistent across models.

It's AI flips the script.
TheyвҖҷve built a decentralized LLM detection subnet where miners compete on accuracy. The network rewards the best performers at spotting AI-generated text from ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Grok, and beyond. 
This incentive-driven approach creates continuous improvement: better models, fewer false positives, and real transparency through on-chain verification.

Key things that impressed me in the research:
- Extremely high accuracy (consistently topping independent benchmarks like ASAP 2.0 with very low false positive rates)
- Sentence-by-sentence deep scans not just an overall percentage, but granular insights into where the AI fingerprints appear
- Practical integrations (Canvas, APIs, enterprise tools) making it usable for educators, publishers, platforms, and teams fighting вҖңAI slopвҖқ
- Strong focus on privacy and decentralization your scans arenвҖҷt feeding another centralized AIвҖҷs training data

In a world drowning in synthetic content, trustworthy detection isnвҖҷt a nice-to-have itвҖҷs infrastructure for truth, trust, and quality online. Pairing this with image/video detection partners (like their recent BitMind collab) creates a full-spectrum integrity layer.

ItвҖҷs AI isnвҖҷt just another detector. ItвҖҷs building the decentralized immune system for the content ecosystem.

If you care about authenticity in the age of agents and generative AI, this is one to watch closely.

Shoutout to the team @ai_detection for pushing the subnet forward.

Hiring вҖјпёҸ
рҹҺҘAI Video Creator
рҹ“ҚRemote
рҹ’ө Salary: N250,000 naira/month

YouвҖҷll be responsible for turning outfit photos into styled videos on one consistent AI model - she must look realistic, not artificial or вҖңAI-generated.вҖқ YouвҖҷll maintain her same face, body, and look across all videos while styling her in different outfits based on briefs and creative direction, without needing a human model or physical shoot.

RequirementsвҖјпёҸ

вҖўProficient in AI video generation tools (e.g., Runway, Kling, Luma, Pika, Sora, or similar)
вҖўSkilled in AI image generation/editing to maintain a consistent, realistic model across videos
вҖўStrong eye for fashion styling, trends, and aesthetics
вҖўAble to follow content briefs and recreate TikTok-style formats and pacing
вҖўStrong attention to detail, clean transitions, consistent lighting/style, no AI artifacts or glitches
вҖўReliable and able to deliver on an agreed content schedule

Expectations:

вҖўMaintain one consistent AI model (same face, body, look) across all content
вҖўEnsure the model looks fully realistic, no obvious or вҖңAI-generatedвҖқ appearance
вҖўEnsure movement, gestures, and expressions look smooth and human-like, not stiff or glitchy

How to Apply:
Send your portfolio/sample AI-generated videos (fashion content) and a list of tools you work with to aivisualmastery@gmail.com

AI app ideas are cheap. Launchable templates are the bottleneck.

Awesome Generative AI Apps is a curated GitHub repo of 50+ open-source generative AI app templates for builders who want to clone, deploy, and adapt AI SaaS products.

It helps you skip the blank Next.js project by grouping templates for image, video, beauty/fashion, e-commerce, writing, agents, audio, and integrations, with setup and deploy paths already documented.

Key features:

вҖў 50+ app templates вҖ“ cloneable products across image generation, video generation, virtual try-on, product photography, content, agents, and more
вҖў SaaS plumbing included вҖ“ README highlights Stripe billing, Google OAuth, Prisma/PostgreSQL, credit balances, and job history
вҖў Vercel deployment path вҖ“ templates point to Deploy to Vercel buttons and a quick-start flow
вҖў Category navigation вҖ“ table of contents lets you scan by use case instead of hunting through random demos
вҖў MIT license вҖ“ repo metadata and README both mark it as MIT licensed for reuse and adaptation

ItвҖҷs open-source (MIT license).

Link in the reply рҹ‘Ү

Andrej Karpathy explains why RLHF needed a cheat code.

The naive version sounds simple: show humans model outputs and ask them to score which ones are good.

Then he does the math:

"I want to do 1,000 updates, where each update will be on 1,000 prompts, and for each prompt we're going to have 1,000 rollouts that we're scoring."

"I will need to ask a human to evaluate a joke a total of 1 billion times."

"That's a lot of people looking at really terrible jokes, so we don't want to do that."

The trick is indirection:

"We're going to involve humans just a little bit, and the way we cheat is that we basically train a whole separate neural network that we call a reward model."

That is the hidden abstraction behind RLHF:

Humans do not scale the feedback loop.
A model trained to imitate human preference scales it.

The new technical report on Krea 2 goes in depth on how to train production-level diffusion models, covering everything from data and infrastructure to RL post-training and distillation.

It also includes extensive ablation studies on design choices, covering recent diffusion model innovations ( TREAD, iREPA, ...), along with extensive architecture ablations inspired by recent language model architectures (GQA, MLA, gated sigmoid attention, ...).

Definitely worth reading!

I repeated same prompts with Opus 4.6, and it worked great.
